chapters.title: The ancient puzzle of how inequality began
chapters.text:
Most people accept that all humans deserve equal treatment, yet the world we live in tells a different story. A small number of people control vast fortunes while others struggle through each day, and many die from hunger or untreated illness.

Where does this unevenness come from? How did certain societies gain the strength to take over much of the planet? What explains the gap between wealthy and poor nations?

A question Jared Diamond has explored is why Native Americans, Africans, and Aboriginal Australians did not end up conquering Europe and Asia instead.

To answer these questions, we have to look far back, well before the year 1500, when colonization was just beginning. By that point, people in Europe, Asia, and North America were already using metal tools and heading toward industry. At the same time, Native Americans still relied on stone tools, and sub-Saharan African communities were working with iron. Across places like Australia and New Guinea, hunting and small-scale farming were still the main way of life.

These differences sit at the root of the imbalance we see today. Groups with stronger materials defeated those who could not defend themselves as well. But what caused the split in the first place? Uneven rates of progress and the reasons behind them hold the answer to how power was gained. The simple truth is that guns, germs, and steel shaped the path of world development. They were needed to survive and to keep building. Figuring out what shaped their rise is the next step in this story.

A clear explanation may help us avoid repeating the same painful errors.

chapters.title: The starting point and the rise of farming
chapters.text:
Pinpointing when inequality began and finding a fair basis for comparison is not easy. Many possible causes could have shaped the path of world development, and untangling them is complex.

Some people resist looking for answers, fearing that explanations might be used to justify racism, slavery, and the violence that flows from power gaps. Nothing could be further from the truth. Understanding the roots of harm does not mean excusing it. What it can do is help us avoid old mistakes.

Several clues suggest why some parts of the world advanced faster than others. One of the most important is agriculture. For most of human history, going back to our split from the great apes, people lived by hunting and gathering. Growing crops and raising animals only began about 11,000 years ago, and not everyone started at the same time. Some groups, like the ancient Chinese, developed farming on their own. Others, like the ancient Egyptians, picked it up from neighbors. A few, including the Aboriginal Australians, never moved to food production at all.

So how does farming connect to guns, germs, and steel? The link is not obvious, yet domesticating plants and animals set the stage for the fates of entire nations. For one thing, the amount of food a region produces shapes its population. The hunting life is risky and unstable, while a working food system lets families count on regular meals. Domestic animals helped people in four main ways, by providing meat, by giving milk, by offering manure for fields, and by pulling plows. They also gave warmth, useful materials, and reliable transport. That comfort gave nations the stability to grow and the strength to win conflicts.

Animals deserve a huge share of the credit for human success.

Animals are also closely tied to germs. People who lived alongside livestock built up resistance to many diseases. When they met groups that had less contact with animals, the second group had weaker defenses. This gap shaped many European conquests, including those of Native Americans and South African peoples.

chapters.title: Why some regions advanced and others did not
chapters.text:
We have already seen how much agriculture can offer, so how do we explain its uneven spread around the world? Looking back at farming history reveals some surprising geographic patterns. Consider California and other US Pacific states, regions with rich soil and good grazing land, yet when European colonizers arrived, the Native Americans there were still hunting and gathering. Another surprise is that the earliest farming sites tend to sit in fairly dry places, such as Iraq, Iran, Mexico, the Andes, parts of China, and the Sahel.

To explain the uneven start of food production, we need to look at the conditions that pushed it forward. Five main factors helped farming take root.

First, wild food sources became harder to find. As humans hunted and gathered over thousands of years, many animal populations shrank, and some species disappeared. This drop happened faster in some regions than in others, which is why the need for steady farming showed up unevenly.

Second, more plants became available for growing. As animal numbers fell, plants offered a rich alternative. A changing climate brought wild grains that grew quickly and gave generous harvests.

Third, tools and techniques kept improving. New inventions let people process, gather, and store food. A large harvest means little if you cannot collect it in time, so better equipment was a key step that let former hunters become farmers.

Fourth, human populations grew. Did more people push the move toward farming, or did farming cause the growth? The honest answer is that the two feed each other. More food supports more people, and more people demand more food.

Fifth, geographic boundaries played a part. As farming populations grew, hunter-gatherers had two choices, adopt the new ways or be pushed aside by the larger farming groups.

The hunting life survived only in places where the land could not support farming or herding.

chapters.title: The path to better tools and weapons
chapters.text:
The next part of the journey is to see how farming led to the inventions that mattered most. Technology, including firearms and steel tools, decides who holds power in the world. It is the main lever for conquering other nations and growing a country. What conditions allow technology to grow? Why did Eurasian peoples invent firearms and steel gear? Why did Native Americans and New Guineans still rely on stone tools in the year 1800, when most other regions had moved on? Why did this happen, even though New Guinea holds some of the largest copper and iron deposits anywhere?

To begin, the same invention can land very differently in different societies. Communities that hold tightly to old ways may reject new tools, even when they would clearly help. The Aboriginal peoples of Northern Australia, for example, could easily have picked up bows and arrows from trading partners in the Torres Straits. That single detail helps explain why some groups kept using stone while better materials sat within reach.

Receptiveness often tracks with geography. Aboriginal Tasmanians were among the most isolated people on earth, and they built their own tools. By contrast, societies in close contact with neighbors swapped ideas and improved quickly.

Location and the spread of technology tend to go together.

Some cases are worth a closer look, such as gaining a new tool and then losing it. The most striking example is Japan, which took up gunmaking from Portuguese travelers and was so impressed that it started building and refining firearms fast. The shift slowed when the ruling samurais, who prized the sword as a true art form, began to limit gun production.

Attitudes toward robots show a similar split between Japan and the United States. In the US, robots often carry a futuristic feel, while in Japan they more often connect to values like respect for elders and harmony. So Japanese robots are often designed to look and act like people, while US designs lean toward efficiency and pure function.

chapters.title: How animal domestication brought deadly germs
chapters.text:
Farming came with a dark companion on the road to world power, which was germs. The big killers of history, including smallpox, flu, plague, and cholera, all began as animal diseases. Germs have taken lives for centuries. Until World War II, infections spread by wartime microbes killed more soldiers than battlefield wounds did.

The most striking example of germ damage is the fate of Native Americans after the Spanish arrived. Conquistadors killed huge numbers with firearms, but the diseases they brought did far more harm. Why did the damage fall in that direction? Why did farming give rise to germs strong enough to wipe out millions?

One estimate Diamond shares is that the Native American population across the New World fell by as much as 95 percent in the century or two after Columbus.

As we have seen, farming allows populations to grow, and more people mean more germs. A settled life also keeps people close to waste. Hunter-gatherers simply moved on and left it behind. Even when waste is used to fertilize fields, it raises exposure to germs. Animal illnesses crossed into human ones, and many died until survivors built up resistance. When Europeans came to North America, they carried the germs their bodies knew. For Native Americans, those same germs meant death.

So why did Europeans not get wiped out by Native American germs? A few reasons help explain it. One is that population density grew far more slowly in the Americas than in Europe. A more important reason ties back to animal domestication. Only five animal species were domesticated in the Americas, which were turkey, alpaca and llama, guinea pig, duck, and dog. None of them carry the range of germs that pigs and cows do.

A turkey is neither as cuddly nor as risky as a lamb.

chapters.title: Writing and the spread of ideas
chapters.text:
Reading and writing are so tightly linked to progress that they almost feel like the same thing. The same is true for knowledge and power. Writing lets people share ideas with great accuracy, and it is the main way to preserve information. New inventions and instructions do little good if they only pass from mouth to mouth.

Writing joined weapons, germs, and steel as a tool of conquest.

Stories of earlier voyages inspired later ones, and travelers knew what to expect because of notes about weather and local habits. Maps and battle plans were central to running a strong fight. Like the other pieces of the puzzle we have looked at, writing did not arrive everywhere at the same time. What caused these gaps, and how did they shape the success of different nations?

In its early form, writing had limited use, and only a few groups of people handled it. It served institutions, keeping track of taxes, treaties, and propaganda. The people doing this work were scribes, and they needed a steady food supply, which only farming societies could provide. Hunter-gatherer groups never took up writing. It held no value for them, since they had no complex governments and no stored food to feed a class of scribes.

Independent writing systems and farming both first appeared in the Fertile Crescent, Mexico, and likely China. Like animal domestication, literacy then spread to neighbors through trade and conquest. That is also why some farming states, such as the Inca Empire, did not develop writing until recent times. They sat too far from the places where writing first grew or was first adopted. As a result, writing moved slowly for some nations, or never arrived at all.

The lack of writing slowed the spread of ideas and information about new tools that states needed to thrive. Without it, a society could not pick up innovations from others or preserve the steps for its own inventions. All of this shaped how well nations fared.

The UNESCO Institute for Statistics has reported that each 1 percent rise in literacy can lift economic growth by about 2.5 percent.

chapters.title: Final thoughts on the forces behind world power
chapters.text:
Inequality is painful, and its grip on the world is a real human tragedy. Nothing happens without reasons, and the unevenness of life is no exception. A long list of factors set some nations up for success and others for hardship. Pulling those factors into view is a key part of understanding world development, and it shows that something as fixed as geography gave certain groups a stronger start and held others back.

Farming played the central role in how power was shared. Food production took hold for many reasons, from the loss of wild resources to the spread of geographic borders. A growing human population, a wider range of plants and animals that could be tamed, and better tools all added to the push for more food.

The rise of farming called for new tools in response. People found better ways to make their work pay off, and those inventions boosted national power even more.

Domesticated animals remain a quiet but vital part of human success. Thanks to close contact with them, people built up resistance to many illnesses and ended up with stronger immune systems.

A small action you can take

Knowing what happened in the past and why gives us the chance to change things now and get better results later.

Can you help shift something? Even tiny details can bend the path of a person's life.

Find a way to join the fight against ongoing disease, hunger, and poverty in parts of the world that need it most. You have the power to leave the world a little better than you found it.

Did you read through to the end?
